title: Body fluid cleanup after mass injury
summary: Focused extreme medical support for body fluid cleanup after mass injury in offline, delayed-help, or collapse conditions.
content: Blood, vomit, feces, and other fluids can spread infection and panic. Cleanup needs barriers, zoning, and disposal before normal activity returns. This is not medical advice; it is a safety and organization checklist until qualified help is available.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Body fluid cleanup after mass injury”.
soap or sanitizer. Context: “Body fluid cleanup after mass injury”.
gloves or clean barrier. Context: “Body fluid cleanup after mass injury”.
paper and pen. Context: “Body fluid cleanup after mass injury”.
phone, radio, or messenger — for «Body fluid cleanup after mass injury»
labels for time and symptoms — for «Body fluid cleanup after mass injury»
steps: Block the area from children, animals, food, and foot traffic.
Wear gloves or the best available barrier.
Use absorbent material and dispose in labeled waste.
Clean hands and contaminated tools afterward.
Ventilate and record if exposure occurred.
warnings: This is not medical advice and it is not a doctor's advice. It is offline emergency organization guidance only. Use local emergency numbers, trained first aid, pharmacists, clinics, doctors, poison centers, and official public-health instructions whenever available. Do not perform surgery, invasive procedures, diagnosis, prescription changes, or medicine dosing from this guide. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Body fluid cleanup after mass injury”.

title: Blood exposure log for helpers
summary: Focused extreme medical support for blood exposure log for helpers in offline, delayed-help, or collapse conditions.
content: Helpers who contact blood or body fluids need a record and professional advice, especially with cuts, eyes, mouth, or needle injury. This is not medical advice; it is a safety and organization checklist until qualified help is available.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Blood exposure log for helpers”.
soap or sanitizer. Context: “Blood exposure log for helpers”.
gloves or clean barrier. Context: “Blood exposure log for helpers”.
paper and pen. Context: “Blood exposure log for helpers”.
phone, radio, or messenger — for «Blood exposure log for helpers»
labels for time and symptoms — for «Blood exposure log for helpers»
steps: Wash skin exposures with soap and clean water.
Flush eye or mouth exposure with clean water if available and safe.
Record time, fluid type, route, wound, and source if known.
Seek professional advice for bloodborne infection risk.
Do not shame the helper; exposure reporting protects everyone.

title: Sterile versus clean limits in collapse
summary: Focused extreme medical support for sterile versus clean limits in collapse in offline, delayed-help, or collapse conditions.
content: Clean is not the same as sterile. This entry prevents false confidence and avoids unsafe invasive care. This is not medical advice; it is a safety and organization checklist until qualified help is available.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Sterile versus clean limits in collapse”.
soap or sanitizer. Context: “Sterile versus clean limits in collapse”.
gloves or clean barrier. Context: “Sterile versus clean limits in collapse”.
paper and pen. Context: “Sterile versus clean limits in collapse”.
phone, radio, or messenger — for «Sterile versus clean limits in collapse»
labels for time and symptoms — for «Sterile versus clean limits in collapse»
steps: Use clean hands, clean water, clean cloth, and clean surfaces for basic care.
Do not call something sterile unless it was properly sterilized and stored.
Do not perform invasive procedures because supplies look clean.
Keep wound care separate from toilets, trash, animals, and cooking.
Refer deep wounds, foreign bodies, severe infection, and surgical needs.
warnings: This is not medical advice and it is not a doctor's advice. It is offline emergency organization guidance only. Use local emergency numbers, trained first aid, pharmacists, clinics, doctors, poison centers, and official public-health instructions whenever available.
